1.synthesis:
David’s direct question — “What have I done? What is my guilt? And what is my sin before your father, that he seeks my life?” (v. 1) — frames the central legal-moral question of the David-Saul conflict. Jonathan initially resists the assessment: my father does nothing great or small without telling me, why would he hide this from me? (v. 2). David’s answer reveals a painful truth: Saul knows Jonathan’s love for David and has concealed his intentions precisely because Jonathan would be distressed (v. 3). Jonathan’s naive confidence in his father’s openness collides with David’s hard-won knowledge of Saul’s intentions.

Together they devise a plan to test Saul’s intentions through the New Moon festival: David will be absent; Jonathan will explain his absence; Saul’s reaction will reveal whether he has genuinely reconciled or still intends David’s death (vv. 5–8). The plan includes a signal system with arrows — Jonathan will shoot arrows and send a boy to retrieve them; the instruction to the boy (“the arrow is beyond you” = flee; or “the arrow is here” = safe) will communicate the verdict without words, allowing David to receive the news without direct exposure to Saul (vv. 20–22). The elaborateness of the plan reflects genuine danger: David and Jonathan are managing a situation where the king is both Jonathan’s father and David’s attempted murderer.
1.historical_context: The New Moon festival was a monthly celebration (Num 10:10; 1 Sam 20:5) involving a feast at which the royal household would gather. David’s absence from such a gathering — a standing royal obligation — would be noticeable and would require explanation. The use of a coded message (arrow placement) as a covert communication system reflects the practical intelligence operations of the ancient court. Jonathan’s designation of David as “go in peace” or “flee” based on arrow direction was deniable — the boy retrieving the arrows would not know the significance of the instruction.
1.literary_structure: Chapter 20 is structured around the central question of Jonathan’s knowledge and choice. The plan they devise is embedded in covenant renewal: before the execution plan is agreed, the two men renew their covenant of mutual loyalty (v. 8, vv. 14–17). The chapter’s emotional arc moves from David’s desperate question to Jonathan’s covenant pledge to the test’s execution and outcome, ending with the farewell scene that is one of the most emotionally charged in the OT. The plan-within-covenant structure shows that what holds the two men together is not just practical alliance but a covenant bond that transcends the political situation.
1.theological_application: David’s question — “what is my sin before your father?” — is a legal question that Jonathan cannot ultimately answer, because the only answer is “nothing.” Saul’s pursuit of David is not a response to David’s sin but to Saul’s own fear, jealousy, and the tormenting spirit. The inability to identify specific guilt in the one being persecuted is a feature of unjust persecution: those who are hunted by the powerful are often hunted not for what they have done but for what they represent. David’s articulation of his own innocence — not as arrogance but as genuine legal puzzlement — models the posture of those who suffer injustice: naming the wrong without manufacturing guilt that does not exist.
1.christology: David’s question “what is my sin?” anticipates the verdict at Christ’s trial: “I find no fault in him” (John 19:4). The persecuted innocent who asks what he has done wrong and receives no coherent answer images the legal farce of the Jerusalem trial — multiple witnesses whose accounts do not agree, a governor who declares innocence and condemns anyway. Jonathan’s painful position — loyal to both father and beloved friend — mirrors the disciples’ position: those who loved Jesus and could not prevent what was coming. Jonathan’s plan is the best human ingenuity can do; it buys time but cannot change Saul’s intentions. Only the resurrection changes what the power of death intends.

12.synthesis:
Jonathan’s covenant speech is a formal covenant ratification that includes mutual obligations across generations. He invokes YHWH as witness: if it becomes clear that Saul intends David’s harm, Jonathan will send David word to go safely (vv. 12–13). Then the covenant turns toward the future: “And may you show me the steadfast love of the LORD, that I may not die; and do not cut off your steadfast love from my house forever, when the LORD cuts off every one of the enemies of David from the face of the earth” (vv. 14–15). Jonathan speaks as if David’s kingship is certain — which it is, in YHWH’s plan — and as if his own death is possible, perhaps likely. He asks for hesed (covenant loyalty) for himself and his house when David is king.

Jonathan renews the covenant with David (“because he loved him as he loved his own soul” — v. 17) and gives him the signal arrangement. The covenant closes with the declaration: “The LORD shall be between me and you, and between my offspring and your offspring, forever” (v. 23). The covenant is multigenerational — it will be fulfilled in 2 Samuel 9 when David seeks out Jonathan’s son Mephibosheth and restores Saul’s lands to him. The man who asks for hesed when he will be gone is speaking prophetically: Jonathan will die before David becomes king, and the covenant he makes here will be honored by the king who outlives him.
12.historical_context: Jonathan’s request for hesed (covenant loyalty) from David is a formal covenant term — the same word used for YHWH’s own covenant faithfulness throughout the Psalms and prophets. The covenant between David and Jonathan was a formal binding agreement with divine witness, not merely a personal friendship promise. The phrase “cut off your steadfast love from my house forever” reflects the ancient practice of new kings eliminating the previous dynasty’s family to prevent future rival claims — Jonathan is asking David not to do this to his descendants. David’s eventual honoring of the covenant in 2 Sam 9 (Mephibosheth brought to Jerusalem and given a seat at the king’s table) is one of the most deliberate acts of covenant-keeping in the OT.
12.literary_structure: The covenant speech of 20:12–23 is embedded between the plan’s devising and the plan’s execution, functioning as the theological heart of the chapter. The covenant ratification precedes the signal system — the formal commitment is established before the practical communication mechanism. The multigenerational horizon of Jonathan’s request (“my offspring and your offspring, forever”) lifts the scene from a crisis-management plan to a covenant that will outlast both participants. Jonathan speaks as the dying man making provision for his descendants; the narrative confirms this implicitly, since the reader knows Jonathan will not live to see David’s coronation.
12.theological_application: Jonathan’s covenant-making in the knowledge of his own likely death is an act of extraordinary faith: he secures the future of his house not through political maneuvering or military protection but through a covenant of hesed with the man YHWH has chosen. He cannot protect his own dynasty; he can only ask the covenant-faithful man who will be king to remember his house. The posture of relinquishing the future to the faithfulness of another — trusting that the covenant-bond will hold what one cannot personally secure — is the posture of faith itself. Communities of faith make similar covenantal investments: promises, dedications, provisions for those who come after, trusting that YHWH’s faithfulness and human covenant-keeping will carry what personal presence cannot.
12.christology: Jonathan’s covenant request — “show me the hesed of the LORD, that I may not die” — is the cry of every human heart for the covenant faithfulness that transcends death. The covenant of YHWH established in Christ is precisely this: the hesed that extends through death to those who are his. Romans 8:38–39: “neither death nor life... will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.” The covenant Jonathan asks David to keep across the generations is the type of the new covenant that Christ keeps across death itself: Mephibosheth at the king’s table despite his disability (2 Sam 9:13) images the sinner made welcome at the King’s table not for his own merit but for the covenant between his ancestor and the King.

24.synthesis:
The New Moon festival unfolds as planned. Day one: David’s place is empty; Saul says nothing, assuming he is ceremonially unclean (v. 26). Day two: David’s place is empty again; Saul asks Jonathan. Jonathan gives the agreed explanation — David asked permission to go to Bethlehem for a family sacrifice (vv. 28–29). Saul’s rage is immediate and uncontrolled. He curses Jonathan in terms that accuse Jonathan’s mother of shame and name Jonathan’s loyalty to David as the instrument of his own shame and his mother’s shame (v. 30). When Jonathan asks what David has done, Saul throws his spear at Jonathan (v. 33). Jonathan leaves the table in fierce anger and grief, knowing that his father has determined to kill David.

The arrow signal is given: Jonathan sends the arrows beyond the boy, David understands, and comes out of hiding to bow three times before Jonathan (v. 41). They kiss each other and weep, David weeping the most. Jonathan’s farewell is a covenant reminder: “Go in peace, because we have sworn both of us in the name of the LORD, saying, ‘The LORD shall be between me and you, and between my offspring and your offspring, forever’” (v. 42). David departs; they will see each other only once more (23:16–18).
24.historical_context: Saul’s curse of Jonathan in v. 30 — “son of a perverse, rebellious woman” — is an honor-shame insult that reflects ANE social dynamics: shaming the son through his mother. The spear thrown at Jonathan mirrors the spears thrown at David — Saul’s violence is indiscriminate when the spirit is on him, reaching even to his own son and heir. The farewell scene at the stone Ezel (if that is the correct reading) is the last substantive meeting between David and Jonathan before Jonathan’s death at Jezreel (31:2). The covenant renewed in tears and covenant language serves as the narrative close of their primary relationship — everything after is consequence.
24.literary_structure: 20:24–42 is the emotional climax of the Jonathan-David narrative. The two-day suspense of the New Moon festival, Saul’s explosion, the arrow signal, and the farewell weeping constitute a complete emotional arc: hope, confirmed fear, careful communication, and anguished parting. The farewell is structured with remarkable precision: three bows, mutual weeping with David weeping the most, the covenant statement, and departure. The narrative decorum of this farewell — the restraint of not narrating the parting in more detail — is exactly right; the scene has said everything and more would diminish it.
24.theological_application: Jonathan’s sending David away with the covenant reminder — “Go in peace; the LORD shall be between us” — models how covenant bonds sustain separation. They cannot stay together; the political situation will not allow it. What they can do is reaffirm what will hold them together even apart: the covenant sworn in YHWH’s name, which extends across distance and time and even death. Communities of faith facing painful separations — whether through conflict, circumstance, or the end of shared ministry — can send each other as Jonathan sends David: with the covenant rather than the relationship as the ground of ongoing connection. What YHWH has bound, separation cannot fully sever.
24.christology: The farewell at the stone Ezel — covenant, weeping, separation with the promise “the LORD between us forever” — anticipates every farewell in the upper room discourse of John 14–16. Jesus’s departing words to the disciples circle around the same tension: I am leaving, but the covenant bond is not broken. “I will not leave you as orphans” (John 14:18). “I am with you always, to the end of the age” (Matt 28:20). Jonathan’s “the LORD shall be between me and you” becomes Christ’s “where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them” (Matt 18:20). The covenant-based presence that outlasts the farewell is the promise both men and the disciples lean on after the parting.
